title.en: Landing Zones Compared: CAF vs Control Tower vs GCP Foundations

description.en: Compare Azure Cloud Adoption Framework landing zones, AWS Control Tower, and Google Cloud foundations — and learn why governance scaffolding must exist before the first workload lands.

story_variants.en:
## The Empty Lot Problem

Nobody builds a factory on an empty lot and adds plumbing later. Yet teams routinely deploy production workloads into a raw cloud subscription, account, or project — then spend two years retrofitting logging, network baselines, and identity boundaries around live traffic. A **landing zone** is the pre-plumbed lot: the opinionated scaffolding of organization structure, guardrails, networking, identity, and logging that exists *before* workload one arrives.

In a multi-cloud enterprise you will build (or inherit) up to three of these, each with its own vocabulary. Your job as architect is to make them *conceptually equivalent* even though they are mechanically different — so that an auditor, a security engineer, or a FinOps analyst can ask the same question of all three estates and get comparable answers.

## The Same Idea, Three Dialects

| Concern | Azure (CAF Landing Zones) | AWS (Control Tower / Organizations) | GCP (Cloud Foundations) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tenancy root | Entra ID tenant + Management Groups | AWS Organization + OUs | Cloud Identity org + Folders |
| Workload container | Subscription | Account | Project |
| Preventive guardrails | Azure Policy (deny/audit effects) | Service Control Policies (SCPs) + Control Tower controls | Organization Policy constraints |
| Identity backbone | Microsoft Entra ID | IAM + IAM Identity Center | Cloud IAM + Cloud Identity |
| Central logging | Log Analytics / Diagnostic settings to central workspace | CloudTrail org trail + centralized S3/CloudWatch | Cloud Audit Logs + log sinks to central project |
| IaC accelerator | Azure Verified Modules / ALZ Bicep & Terraform | Control Tower + Account Factory for Terraform (AFT) | Fabric FAST / terraform-google-modules foundation |

> **Callout:** Memorize the container mapping — **Subscription ↔ Account ↔ Project** — and the guardrail mapping — **Azure Policy ↔ SCP ↔ Org Policy**. Half of all cross-cloud governance conversations are just translating between these columns.

## What a Real Landing Zone Contains

Regardless of provider, an enterprise-grade landing zone delivers:

- **Hierarchy with intent.** Management groups / OUs / folders that encode *policy inheritance*, not org charts. A common shape: platform (connectivity, identity, management) vs landing zones (corp, online), plus sandbox and decommissioned branches.
- **Preventive controls at the top.** Deny public storage exposure, deny disabling audit logs, restrict regions to an approved list, require encryption. Preventive beats detective: a control that blocks the action at the API is worth ten dashboards.
- **Centralized, tamper-resistant logging.** Audit trails shipped out of workload containers into an account/subscription/project that workload teams cannot write to.
- **Network baseline.** Hub-and-spoke (Azure), Transit Gateway-centered (AWS), or Shared VPC (GCP) — with egress inspection points decided up front.
- **Vending machine.** New subscriptions/accounts/projects are *manufactured* by pipeline (subscription vending, Account Factory, project factory), landing pre-wired with guardrails, tags, budgets, and network attachments.

## Where the Three Diverge in Practice

- **Azure** leans on *policy-as-governance*: Azure Policy is unusually expressive (deployIfNotExists can auto-remediate), and management groups make inheritance natural. The trap: policy sprawl — hundreds of assignments nobody can reason about.
- **AWS** separates *authorization ceiling* (SCPs never grant, only constrain) from IAM grants. Control Tower gives a curated starting point, but large enterprises frequently outgrow its opinions and drive everything through Organizations + Terraform (AFT). The trap: SCP debugging is famously opaque — an action denied by an SCP looks like a permissions bug to the workload team.
- **GCP** benefits from a genuinely hierarchical resource model — org → folder → project — with Organization Policy constraints that are simpler but less expressive than Azure Policy. The trap: teams treating projects as free-for-all sandboxes because project creation is so lightweight.

## Failure Modes That Cost Real Money

- **Retrofit governance.** Applying a deny-public-storage policy *after* 400 storage accounts exist means months of exception triage. Landing zones exist precisely to avoid governing in arrears.
- **The single giant container.** One subscription/account/project holding prod, dev, and the intern's experiment. Blast radius, quota contention, and cost attribution all collapse.
- **Asymmetric guardrails.** The anchor cloud has 40 controls; the secondary cloud (10% of spend) has 4. Attackers and auditors both gravitate to the weak estate. Your cross-cloud control matrix must be *capability-equivalent*, even if implementations differ.
- **Manual vending.** If a human clicks new environments into existence, every environment is a snowflake. The vending pipeline *is* the landing zone in operational terms.

> **Callout:** A landing zone is never "done." Treat it as a product with a roadmap, versioned IaC, and a change process — the alternative is governance that fossilizes while the provider ships 200 new services a year.

## Cross-Cloud Equivalence as a Deliverable

Enterprise security teams increasingly maintain a **control matrix**: one row per control objective (e.g., "audit logs centralized and immutable", "MFA enforced for privileged roles", "public network exposure denied by default"), with three columns showing how each cloud implements it and its current status. This artifact is what turns three landing zones into one governable estate — and it is exactly what you will simulate in this lesson's challenge: a guardrail compliance checker that reports, per account, which required controls are missing.

description.en: Five pillars every landing zone must deliver before the first workload arrives — on any provider.

left.label: Deploy first, retrofit policies around live traffic later

right.label: Vend pre-governed containers: guardrails, logging, budgets on day zero

description.en: Governing in arrears means months of exception triage. Vending machines manufacture compliant environments instead.
Policy flows down the hierarchy: controls attached high are inherited by every container below.

explanation.en: The control matrix: one canonical list of control objectives, implemented as Azure Policy, SCPs, or Org Policy per cloud.
Each account/subscription/project is checked against the same canonical list — capability equivalence across clouds.
Sorted output makes the report deterministic and diff-able in audit evidence.

description.en: The audit output security teams actually want: per-container gaps against one canonical control list.

instruction.en: Build a landing-zone guardrail auditor. Given REQUIRED_GUARDRAILS and a list of accounts each with a 'guardrails' list, implement missing_guardrails(account) returning a SORTED list of required guardrails the account lacks. For each account (in order) print exactly '<name>: COMPLIANT' if nothing is missing, otherwise '<name>: MISSING <g1>,<g2>,...' (sorted, comma-joined, no spaces after commas). Finally print 'Compliant accounts: <n>/<total>'. This is the control-matrix check auditors run across Subscriptions, Accounts, and Projects alike.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
REQUIRED_GUARDRAILS = ["mfa_enforced", "central_logging", "network_baseline", "budget_alerts", "deny_public_storage"]

ACCOUNTS = [
    {"name": "prod", "guardrails": ["mfa_enforced", "central_logging", "network_baseline", "budget_alerts", "deny_public_storage"]},
    {"name": "dev", "guardrails": ["mfa_enforced", "central_logging", "network_baseline"]},
    {"name": "sandbox", "guardrails": ["mfa_enforced"]},
]

def missing_guardrails(account):
    # TODO: return sorted list of REQUIRED_GUARDRAILS not present in account['guardrails']
    pass

# TODO: for each account print '<name>: COMPLIANT' or '<name>: MISSING <sorted,comma,joined>'
# TODO: print 'Compliant accounts: <n>/<total>'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
prod: COMPLIANT
dev: MISSING budget_alerts,deny_public_storage
sandbox: MISSING budget_alerts,central_logging,deny_public_storage,network_baseline
Compliant accounts: 1/3

en: Convert account['guardrails'] to a set, then collect required items not in that set and sort them.
Use ','.join(missing) — no spaces — to build the MISSING line.
Count accounts where missing_guardrails returns an empty list, then print f'Compliant accounts: {n}/{len(ACCOUNTS)}'.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert missing_guardrails({"name": "x", "guardrails": []}) == sorted(REQUIRED_GUARDRAILS)
assert missing_guardrails({"name": "x", "guardrails": list(REQUIRED_GUARDRAILS)}) == []
assert missing_guardrails({"name": "x", "guardrails": ["central_logging", "mfa_enforced", "network_baseline"]}) == ["budget_alerts", "deny_public_storage"]
assert isinstance(missing_guardrails({"name": "x", "guardrails": ["mfa_enforced"]}), list)

q.en: Which mapping correctly pairs the workload containers across clouds?
What is the AWS analog of Azure Policy deny effects and GCP Organization Policy constraints?
Why do preventive controls beat detective controls in a landing zone?

en: They block the non-compliant action at the API instead of reporting it after the fact

q.en: An enterprise has 40 guardrails on its anchor cloud and 4 on its secondary cloud. What is the core problem?

en: Nothing — small footprints need fewer controls
Auditors only review the anchor cloud
Attackers and auditors both find the weak estate; controls must be capability-equivalent across clouds
